speaker: interviewer
text: Sally Huppert, tape number 3. You were talking about your sister in-- in Auschwitz. And did you say that she got scarlet fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came to Brunnlitz, and she got scarlet fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But you said that in Auschwitz she was sick, and you stole--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sick, She was sickly. I didn't know. I went out, and I stole a-- a scarf, you know, I got hit from the sturmfuhrer. The girl what is taking care-- the German girl. I had saw this-- I should put on some-- she should put on something, a scarf, woolen scarf I had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when you-- we didn't have [? where ?] to steal, only sometime when they are going to the kitchen I find out this-- find on the floor a cabbage. And when everybody was asleep, I-- I gave everyone, I gave a leaf of cabbage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not everyone. How many was in that neighborhood, how much I had. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You said when you took the scarf, you were beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beaten by sturmbannfuhrer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my head, on my back, with a [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: A pipe?

speaker: interviewee
text: A [NON-ENGLISH] a leather [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A pouch, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know this has, you hit like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And how long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. Schindler came and pick us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him come to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were talking about he came, and he probably gave money. And he took us to-- to the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like when you left Auschwitz? I mean, you know, getting on-- how did you travel leaving Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leaving Auschwitz, you went-- everybody, when we came to Auschwitz, we went-- went in-- like cows in a wagon. When we came to Auschwitz, and when we went. It doesn't matter. We have to-- we went to Auschwitz or to Czechoslovakia. We were very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was that journey about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] train arrived in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the factory, to the barrack we got there, all of us. We'd look for perches where we're going to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a room, in a big room. We had showers. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said your sister got sick when you arrived in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She had scarlet fever. And she was sick for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of care did she get when she was sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They took her to the hospital. They take of here, because she get well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did you work in Brunnlitz also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], this ammunition. I measured this. This was just for taking time. That Schindler he was smart. He knows. Just for the time when-- when we-- we got out. It was the plan they're gonna kill us there in Auschwitz, in Czechoslovakia. A mass grave they make already. And they tell us, somebody tell me, from the office. But it didn't go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who made the mass grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This was a lagerfuhrer a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brunnlitz. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But I think Schindler took care of this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Banker, Schindler without banker, banker tell him everything what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who really ran the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever encounter Emilie Schindler when you were in Brunnlitz, Schindler's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I know her for a long time. But I don't-- I don't-- I don't know nothing. They write about her. She had a-- he had a girlfriend, Paula. And he wrote to the banker, he should take of-- banker should take of his girlfriend. But banker couldn't-- couldn't help-- he couldn't do nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear they talk about Mrs. Schindler, she was so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't really have any contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just knew who she was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you guarded? What were the guards like in-- in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sturmbann girls, but nothing-- just I went to the toilet, and it was sunny beautiful. So this sun, I wanted, I ask her, can I stay here for a while? She said, no sun is for Jews. No sun is for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was just outside of the factory or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waiting for the toilet in line. You're not gonna have any sun anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any violence at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were-- how were you fed? Was-- was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We got bread and soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have contact with people from the area at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know-- once you were in Brunnlitz, were you aware of the progress of the war and what was happening outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They say not long, not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: One German was walking with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked with Germans as well as Jews, is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: No he was-- he was ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the day that the camp was liberated, or right before the camp was liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian came, and they were talking and talking, till they get-- we get the soup. We were so hungry. And they talk-- there were all this talking a lot at first.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians were talking to you or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Because they were Russians, Russians. I didn't remember-- and I didn't understand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- for transport I was waiting, we should go back to Poland. I couldn't wait. So I went-- I went to [? Praga-- ?] Prague. I must tell you, the people there were wonderful. They give us, nuns, they give us rooms in the-- in the places, and they give us food five times a week-- a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were so nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back for a moment to Brunnlitz, did you see Oskar Schindler leave Brunnlitz? Were you aware that he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was not away-- aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there once the Russians arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you treated by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have nothing-- no contact with me. I was afraid of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you went to Prague from Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Prague in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you go? How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. I have a card I am going from concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who gave you that card?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you from Brunnlitz to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: A girlfriend, one girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go when you arrived in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was people waiting for us. They were waiting. They took us to-- which to the nuns. And they give me a-- a room-- not a room, a lot of people together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in a convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a convent, the nuns-- nuns they were so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a week. I wanted to go to Krakow. I wanted to see who's alive, and who's-- so I couldn't wait to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone or with someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find when you arrived in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Empty, nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to Rabka. Oh, it was terrible. I don't remember what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you went to Rabka from Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, by-- by car. I went-- I hitchhike. And I hitchhike a German car, with my sister. And they took us to Rabka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find when you arrived there? Did you go to your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to our apartment I go. I found nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you search for your-- the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through people, just through people. I don't know what happened to my sister, Necha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you mentioned before that you had forgotten to-- to say something about Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, all this news, they killed my sister's boyfriend. He went out for the paper. And they kill-- who else did they kill? Yeah, [INAUDIBLE], a girl, she was walking there. And they kill her. They, after work, they tell her to stay. And she was a sickly girl. And Rosenbaum kill her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness that or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Next thing we know, she-- he was-- he killed her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went back to Rabka, how were you treated by the non-Jews, the local population there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The landlord, what-- who was our landlord, he-- I find out-- he kill-- he sent to the gestapo my mother. I didn't tell you about. My mother sent to-- my-- my sister to him, she was ask him, she wanted to ask him, I left our apartment everything. I left-- he should give us some money for bread. He didn't want it. He took my sister, and put-- send her to-- to gestapo. Now-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he sent her to gestapo, and the-- the gestapo killed her. They said, she had to sign a paper, she was a-- a Russian spy. She was 16. She didn't know nothing about anything. She just was reading and studying. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They killed her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so beautiful she was, so beautiful, blonde. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later, my mother came to look for my sister. He took my mother, and he took her to-- sent her to-- to gestapo. He bring her to gestapo. And they kill her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that happened when you were in the forest, outside of Rabka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long-- excuse me-- how long did you stay in Rabka after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. I ran away from there. I couldn't stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get work in Krakow after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I went to friends. But they went-- what [INAUDIBLE]. Yes. I went to my friends. I met my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through my friends, I meet my husband. We went to Germany, and I got married. That's all. And I went to Konstanz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Regensburg, and Regensburg is when I went to Konstanz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But back in Krakow, you said you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name and where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was from Peterswaldau He was from German town, in-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Max Huppert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He called Maximilian. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you marry in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I marry in Krakow. Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your wedding like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's room, a rabbi, Levertov, was in our-- in our-- in our camp. And I was married by a rabbi. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Rabbi Levertov married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levertov. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my-- from camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was banker's friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Krakow after you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, right away after I got married, I ran away from Krakow. I didn't want to stay there. My sister was married to banker. Before I came, she got married to banker. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't have no home. So I went-- they have a quota. They say if you come in time, you can take care of things. I don't know. My husband say, let's go. We went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you leave Poland? How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Travel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Poland already occupied by the Russians at that point? Were there any restrictions on leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was. It was-- I don't know. I don't remember. I went by car, color people, took me to-- to Konstanz not to Konstanz to Regensburg, Germany-- the German zone. We traveled-- we're hiding at night. I remember. And the English people took us. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel with a lot of people or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In this truck, with color people.

speaker: interviewer
text: With what people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Color people, probably American.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They were very nice. They were hiding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- was it just you and your husband, or were other people with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, more people. More people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? You went to Regensburg, you said, Regensburg in Germany, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I said. I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Regensburg, yeah, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, maybe a week or two. And we met a boy, a young boy. He said, go to Konstanz. It's a Swiss-- it's not far from Switzerland. And I went to Konstanz. And I was working in Switzerland in a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you used to go from Konstanz to Switzerland daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your husband do in Konstanz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Konstanz, he was a gardener. He learned to be a gardener.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Konstanz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but kind of a home did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have a home. I have a room by a German girl, a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- I was pregnant, and I got a baby there, in Konstanz. Is first thing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mary Ann was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to-- come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have family in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband had no family here either?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how did you travel from Konstanz to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you seasick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby was fine. She was drinking milk and eating crackers. But I was very sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a boat, the [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember to day. This I didn't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a boat was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was for soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you arrive when you arrived in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was very happy. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was thinking here is-- it's not anti-Semitism, it's not this, I was thinking. I was very disappointed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were disappointed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very happy, but-- this no-- I was thinking there's not anti-Semitism. It was plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism when you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From Joint, a woman came. And she was very nice, very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she met-- did she meet you the boat or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was American, from the Joint.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live when you first came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Downtown, I took-- I found an apartment for $18. I lived there. Jefferson Street in New York, downtown. Oh, it was horrible. The mouse were running like crazy in the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not used to it. In Germany, I lived-- in Konstanz, I lived in like a nice-- nice apartment I have. And here I was thinking it's going to be beautiful. It's going to be-- it was very tough [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till I go out from there. I move to-- here in Jackson Heights. And now you know where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you first came to this country, did people ask you about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not much, and I was very nervous, very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe they asked me. I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you experienced some anti-Semitism when you came here, or no? No. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I have no contact with nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
